segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an animated depiction of Earth from space, showing a satellite in orbit sending quantum communication beams to two ground locations, one marked 'Eavesdropper' and another unnamed station, while a narrator explains, "The satellite sends entangled photons to two users." Tense, sci-fi-like background music plays. A red glow appears around the 'Eavesdropper' ground station, visually representing an attempt to intercept the signal, which is then thwarted as the beam continues to its intended destination. The narrator elaborates, "An eavesdropper could intercept one of the entangled photons, measure it, and send on a replacement photon." He clarifies the security mechanism, stating, "But it wouldn't be an entangled photon. Its properties wouldn't match. It would be clear an eavesdropper was on the line," as the animated beams continue to illustrate secure transmission. The narrator concludes this animated sequence by explaining, "In theory, this technique could be used to create a totally secure global communication network." The scene transitions to Jian-Wei Pan, identified by on-screen text as from the University of Science and Technology of China, speaking in an office with scientific diagrams in the background. He explains, with a noticeable accent, "So the next step is we will have ground station, for example, in Canada, and also in Africa, and many countries." The background music softens slightly as Pan continues, "So we will use our satellite for the global quantum communication," and emphasizes, "We want to push this technology as far as possible." The video then cuts to a time-lapse of a large, white ground-based telescope at night, housed inside an observatory, emitting red and green laser beams into the sky, likely for satellite communication. This is followed by an animated map of China, showing a complex network of yellow connections and red beams linking ground stations to a satellite, with Chinese text '量子科学实验卫星' (Quantum Science Experiment Satellite) indicating a quantum communication network. The narrator states, "These are the first steps in creating a completely unhackable quantum internet of the future." The sci-fi music intensifies as the narrator continues, "Made possible by quantum entanglement," while the animated map persists. The scene then transitions to a close-up of a dimly lit laboratory filled with complex optical equipment, glowing red lights, and intricate machinery. The narrator concludes with a dramatic shift, "But there's a problem," accompanied by a low, tense sound effect, leaving the segment on a cliffhanger.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark, abstract animation of glowing red spheres, accompanied by a mysterious, low synth hum, as a male narrator questions, "What if quantum entanglement, spooky action at a distance, isn't real after all?" He continues, suggesting it "could mean entangled photons are not the path to complete security." The scene transitions to a modern control room where operators monitor a large digital display showing a real-time quantum communication network map of China, highlighting cities like Beijing, Shanghai, and Hefei, and the 'Waigaoqiao Data Center'. The narrative then shifts to historical black-and-white photographs of early quantum experiments, including a young man in a lab with complex machinery, overlaid with mathematical equations and scientific text. A key document, 'Can Quantum-Mechanical Description of Physical Reality Be Considered Complete?' by Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen, is shown alongside a diagram of a source emitting pairs of photons, as the narrator explains, "The question goes back to Clauser and Freedman's Bell test experiment." He elaborates that "In the years after their pioneering work, physicists began to test possible loopholes in their experiment," describing "ways in which the illusion of entanglement might be created," so "the effect might not be so spooky after all," noting that "one loophole is especially hard to rule out." The video then presents an animated diagram of a quantum experiment setup, where a central 'Source' emits particles to two 'Detectors' on either side, each preceded by a 'Filter', visually demonstrating quantum non-locality as the detectors light up in correlation. The narrator explains, "In modern Bell test experiments, devices at each side test whether the photons can pass through one of two filters that are randomly chosen, effectively asking one of two questions." The PBS | NOVA logo remains visible throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with an animated diagram illustrating a quantum experiment setup, featuring a central 'Source' emitting light or particles to two 'Detectors' on either side, each preceded by a 'Filter' with two adjustable components. The diagram, overlaid on a dark, atmospheric background with glowing, nebula-like effects, dynamically shows filters switching positions and detectors lighting up, as a male narrator explains the process of "checking how often the answers agree" and how, if results show "more agreement than Einstein's physics predicts," the particles "must be spookily entangled." The narrator then questions, "But what if something had mysteriously influenced the equipment so that the choices of the filters were not truly random?" The scene transitions to a close-up of David Kaiser, identified as 'DAVID KAISER MIT', speaking expressively in a lecture hall with chalkboards in the background. With an articulate voice, he gestures as he asks, "Is there any common cause, deep in the past... that could have nudged the questions to be asked and the types of particles to be emitted?" He suggests that what "looks like entanglement might indeed be an accident, an illusion," and that "maybe the world still acts like Einstein thought." The animated diagram briefly reappears, now with the source emitting a bright horizontal beam, emphasizing the quantum connection. The video then cuts to a sweeping aerial view of a misty mountain landscape, before revealing a high-altitude observatory perched atop a rocky ridge, surrounded by fog and distant mountains under a partly cloudy sky. Over swelling, slightly dramatic background music, the male narrator concludes, "It is this loophole that the team at the high-altitude observatory in the Canary Islands is working to tackle."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a sweeping aerial view of a remote, high-altitude mountainous observatory complex, featuring multiple white domes perched above a sea of clouds under a clear blue sky, accompanied by a dramatic, slightly mysterious orchestral background music. A narrator begins, stating, "With quantum mechanics now more established than ever..." as the scene transitions inside the TNG Telescope, where researchers observe its dome rotating, with 'TNG' clearly visible. The narrator continues, "...they're determined to put entanglement to the ultimate test and finally settle the Einstein-Bohr debate beyond all reasonable doubt." A man in a beanie and glasses works with equipment, followed by a close-up of a computer screen displaying a star chart and data. The narrator explains, "The team is creating a giant version of Clauser and Freedman's Bell test, with the entire universe as their lab bench," as a scientist in a blue shirt adjusts intricate optical equipment in a dimly lit lab, where a green laser beam is visible. The video then returns to the mountain landscape, now transitioning from day to night with stars appearing, while the background music continues. The narrator introduces, "In this cosmic Bell test..." as an animated diagram illustrates a quantum experiment: a 'Source' emitting particles detected by two 'Detectors' on either side. The narrator details, "The source of the entangled particles is about a third of a mile from each of the detectors. The team must send perfectly timed pairs of photons through the air to each side." The diagram animates with glowing nebula-like effects. The narrator adds, "At the same time, the telescopes will collect light from two extremely far off, extremely bright galaxies called quasars," as the diagram evolves into a cosmic visualization of a black hole with an accretion disk and powerful jets. The narrator concludes, "These are among the brightest objects in the sky, emitting..." The PBS | NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a starry night sky over a mountainous silhouette, where two bright stars are connected by dashed lines to two glowing points on the horizon, symbolizing a scientific observation setup. Diagrams of numbered filters appear beside the glowing points, suggesting a quantum experiment, while a male narrator explains, "light in powerful jets. Random variations in this light will control which filters are used to measure the photon pairs," accompanied by atmospheric, scientific background music. The scene transitions to a sunrise over a sea of clouds, revealing a large observatory dome on a mountain peak, emphasizing its high-altitude location. The camera pans across the landscape, showing multiple domes and the vast cloud layer below, creating a serene and isolated atmosphere. The narrator continues, "And since the quasars are so far away, the light has been traveling for billions of years to reach Earth. It makes it incredibly unlikely that anything could be influencing the random nature of the test. If the experiment is successful, the team will have tackled the loophole and shown that quantum entanglement is as spooky as Bohr always claimed." A brief female voice is heard saying "Das ist noch nicht weit." The video then splits into two screens: on the left, a telescope is shown on the mountain, while on the right, a man in a gray shirt is seen in a control room, speaking into a phone. The narrator introduces, "Dominic and Jason are at one telescope. Anton is at the other," followed by a male voice saying "Hello Anton." The split screen continues, showing a wider view of the control room with multiple operators at desks, while the observatory dome is visible on the right. The man in the gray shirt is then shown in close-up, speaking into the phone with subtitles displaying his German dialogue: "Wir hatten gerade schon den Quasar im field of view oder in der Iris." (We just had the quasar in the field of view or in the iris.) A colleague's voice asks in German, "Mit der Quelle funktioniert's auch?" (Does it also work with the source?), to which the man in the gray shirt confirms, "Ja, Quelle funktioniert, der Kontrast ist super." (Yes, the source is working, the contrast is great.) The colleague responds, "Läuft." (It's running.), and after the man in gray shirt says "Okay," they exchange "Ciao." The scene shifts to a close-up of a laptop and phone on a desk, with the man in a green jacket leaning over, focused on the screen. The final shot is an interior view of a large telescope inside its dome, with the telescope’s structure illuminated from below, highlighting its intricate design and readiness for observation, as the atmospheric music swells and fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide, cinematic shot of a mountain-top observatory at twilight, its multiple domed telescopes silhouetted against a dusky sky, accompanied by ethereal, atmospheric music. A large, modern TNG Telescope, housed in a ribbed, metallic observatory structure, is shown as its dome slowly opens, revealing the instrument inside. A calm, authoritative male narrator states, "With clear skies finally overhead, the huge telescopes awaken," and later, "Poised to collect light from distant quasars," as the sky darkens and the observatory is illuminated, emphasizing its scientific purpose. The scene transitions to a control room where a team of scientists, including Dominik Rauch from the University of Vienna, intensely monitor data on computer screens. One screen displays a starfield with a green targeting circle, while another shows a 'THOR' software interface. Dialogue begins with a male voice asking, "Dominik, is this the dark count level or is the mirror engaged?", to which another responds, "Dark count level." Dominik, with a slight accent, confirms, "Okay, this is good now," as a colleague explains they are "doing everything at once now," with "the guys for the links... setting the state of the entangled photon pair" to "acquire the quasar." The team discusses "centering it and making the field of view as small as possible to be sure that we only have the quasar." Dominik excitedly asks, "It's guiding now?", receiving an affirmative response, and then exclaims, "Alright. Great, great, great, great, great," visually reacting with excitement and surprise, covering his mouth. Close-ups show hands adjusting equipment and laptops displaying live telemetry, while Jason Gallicchio from Harvey Mudd College works at a separate station. The colleague concludes, "Yeah, that's good. Looks like 90, let's say 91," as the atmosphere of high concentration and scientific discovery persists.

The camera captures close-ups of hands adjusting equipment, laptops displaying live telemetry, and the team’s focused expressions. The scene then cuts to Jason Gallicchio from Harvey Mudd College, working at a separate station, analyzing data on his laptop. The atmosphere is one of high concentration and scientific discovery, underscored by the remote, high-altitude setting of the observatory and the collaborative effort in the control room.

He interacts with a laptop and phone, his expression shifting from concentration to excitement. A calm male narrator explains, "With the telescopes now locked on to two different quasars, the team begins to take readings." Dominik's focused voice is heard reading data, "Let's count 12,000. The scene transitions to a European cityscape at dusk, showcasing red-tiled rooftops, spires, and a prominent green dome, as the music becomes more ambient and ethereal. The camera then focuses on a classical building, the 'INSTITUT FÜR RADIUMFORSCHUNG.' Inside, the ambient music fades as a male narrator states, "Two months later, back in Vienna, the team analyzes the experimental data." Two men are seated at a desk, with subtle keyboard clicks audible, analyzing 'Cosmic Bell Test Data Analysis' on a computer screen. One man points at the screen, which displays graphs and numerical results including an 'S value' of 2.6734. The Man in Gray Shirt, with a slight accent, remarks, "This might take a second. The numbers look really great and it is extremely pleasing to see that all this worked so nice. We clearly see correlations that correspond to quantum mechanics." The narrator then declares, "The results show entanglement," as the sci-fi synth music returns dramatically. The scene shifts to a dynamic animation of a black hole with swirling matter and powerful jets, followed by a return to an observatory, now overlaid with animated beams from two bright stars connecting to ground-based detectors, symbolizing a cosmic experiment. The final frames show the detectors illuminated, with filters labeled '1' and '2' indicating measurement settings, visually representing the quantum entanglement experiment being analyzed, while the narrator explains that "since the light from the quasars controlling the test was nearly 8 billion years old, it's extremely unlikely that anything could have affected its random nature."

A male narrator begins, explaining that quantum concepts have "profound implications," meaning "we must accept that an action in one place can have an instant effect anywhere in the universe, as if there's no space between them." The scene transitions to a close-up of hands performing a card trick, where a playing card is manipulated and seemingly transformed into a coin, showcasing dexterity and illusion. The narrator continues, adding that particles might "only take on physical properties when we observe them," or "we must accept both." The focus then shifts to a man in a vintage suit with a bow tie, who performs a shell game with two wooden cups concealing glowing light sources, emphasizing mystery and hidden elements. This is followed by Shohini Ghose, a woman in a blue top from Wilfrid Laurier University, who speaks directly to the camera while demonstrating dice rolling, symbolizing probability and randomness. She articulates that "we're left with conclusions about the universe that make no sense whatsoever," stating that "science is stepping outside of all of our boundaries of common sense," and likening it to "being in Alice in Wonderland, right? Where everything is possible." The narrative then cuts to a montage of historical scientific imagery, including sepia-toned photographs of physicists, handwritten equations, and diagrams of atomic models, interwoven with modern scientific visuals like quantum experiment schematics and a glowing nebula, suggesting a bridge between past and present scientific inquiry. The male narrator returns, explaining that this phenomenon "was first seen as an unwelcome but unavoidable consequence of quantum mechanics," and now, "after nearly a century of disputes and discoveries, spooky action at a distance is finally at the heart..." The segment concludes with a serene outdoor shot of a university campus, featuring bare trees and a red-brick academic building, grounding the abstract concepts in a real-world educational setting, all set to a subtle, evolving background score. The PBS | NOVA logo is visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a large, red-brick academic building with a clock tower on a grassy campus under an overcast sky, as ethereal, slightly mysterious background music begins. A male narrator introduces the topic of "modern physics," continuing to explain that at the "Institute for Advanced Study," where "the concept of entanglement was first described," researchers are now using it in their search for "a single unified theory of the universe." This transitions to a close-up of a historical document titled 'The EINSTEIN PODOLSKY ROSEN PARADOX,' overlaid with faded images of Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen, and scientific diagrams, emphasizing the foundational quantum theory being explored. A man in a blue suit is then seen walking along a stone path on the campus, holding a coffee cup, with patches of snow visible, suggesting a modern-day academic in a cold season. The video cuts to a breathtaking time-lapse of a starry night sky, with stars streaking across the heavens above a rocky outcrop reflected in still water, creating a sense of cosmic wonder. This is followed by a split-screen view: the left side shows the starry sky, while the right side displays abstract, glowing orbs floating in a dark, nebulous space, symbolizing quantum particles. The narrator explains that "Einstein's theories of special and general relativity perfectly describe space, time, and gravity at the largest scales of the universe," while "quantum mechanics perfectly describes the tiniest scales," noting that "these two theories have never been brought together." The scene transitions to Sean Carroll from Caltech, speaking directly to the camera in a room with bookshelves, gesturing as he explains that "we've not yet had a single complete theory that is both quantum mechanical and reproduces the prediction of Einstein's wonderful theory of general relativity," before suggesting, "Maybe the secret is entanglement." The final shot returns to the cosmic theme, showing a close-up of glowing, translucent spheres—possibly representing atoms or quantum particles—floating in a blue, ethereal environment, reinforcing the theme of quantum physics and the universe’s fundamental nature.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an abstract animation of glowing blue and green spheres floating in a teal, fluid-like environment, accompanied by ambient, sci-fi-like electronic music. A calm male narrator's voice asks, "What if space itself is actually created by the tiny quantum world?" This transitions to a split-screen view, with the sphere animation on the left and a thermal image of a steaming kettle on the right, as the narrator explains, "Just like temperature, warm and cold, consists simply of the movement of atoms inside an object." A vibrant, dynamic network animation composed of multicolored lines and nodes then pulses on screen, while the narrator continues, "Perhaps space as we know it, emerges from networks of entangled quantum particles. It's a mind-blowing idea." The scene shifts outdoors to a grassy campus setting where Robbert Dijkgraaf, identified by on-screen text as from the Institute for Advanced Study, stands beside a large black chalkboard. Speaking with a distinct accent, he states, "What we are learning these days is that we might have to give up that what Einstein held sacred, namely space and time. So, he was always thinking, well, we have little pieces of space and time and out of this we build the whole universe." As he gestures and begins drawing with white chalk—first a simple cube, then a large circle with a dashed line, and finally a vertical line connecting them to form a hot air balloon-like diagram—the narrator's voice returns to describe "a radical theory, known as the holographic universe. Space and time are created by entangled quantum particles on a sphere that's infinitely far away." Robbert continues drawing and explaining, adding, "What's happening in space in some sense all..." The camera alternates between close-ups of his hand drawing and wider shots of him explaining the diagram, with the PBS | NOVA logo consistently visible in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Robbert Dijkgraaf’s hands as he draws a hot air balloon-like diagram with a suspended cube on a large outdoor chalkboard using white chalk. He explains in a clear, non-American accent that what's "described in terms of a screen outside here," is where "the ultimate description of reality resides." He gestures, elaborating that we should "think of it as kind of quantum bits living on that screen," and that "this, like a movie projector, creates an illusion of the three-dimensional reality that I'm now experiencing." The camera pulls back to show him in a grassy area beside a brick building under an overcast sky. A dramatic musical score begins as the scene transitions to a dark, atmospheric animation where a glowing, star-filled nebula condenses into a rotating, luminous sphere resembling Earth. A narrator, with a formal American accent, states, "It may be impossible to intuitively understand this wild mathematical idea, but it suggests that entanglement could be what forms the true fabric of the universe." The animation fades back to Robbert Dijkgraaf at the chalkboard, who continues, "The most puzzling element of entanglement, that somehow two points in space can communicate, becomes less of a problem because space itself has disappeared." The video then cuts to a vibrant, abstract animation of a colorful, interconnected network of lines and nodes, resembling a neural or quantum network, as Robbert concludes, "In the end, we just have this quantum mechanical world, there is no space anymore. And so in some sense the paradoxes..." before returning to him speaking and gesturing beside the chalkboard. The PBS | NOVA logo remains visible in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Robbert Dijkgraaf, wearing a blue suit and light blue shirt, standing outdoors beside a large black chalkboard, drawing a diagram of a hot air balloon with a cube suspended beneath it using white chalk, as he explains with a slight accent that "the EPR paradox disappears into thin air." The scene transitions to a dimly lit laboratory filled with complex optical equipment, where red laser lights pulse and reflect off mirrors and lenses, creating a dramatic, high-tech atmosphere, accompanied by atmospheric, slightly dramatic music. The camera focuses on glowing red spheres, possibly representing particles or data points, floating in darkness. Sean Carroll, in a dark blazer over a light gray shirt, is then seen seated in a room with bookshelves, speaking expressively and gesturing with his hands, explaining that "truly understanding quantum mechanics will only happen when we put ourselves on the entanglement side, when we stop privileging the world that we see, and start thinking about the world as it actually is," with the atmospheric music continuing. Next, a high-speed automated machine is shown assembling electronic components with precision, its robotic arms moving swiftly over a yellow and black circuit board, as the dramatic music swells slightly. The video then cuts to David Kaiser, a bald man in a brown suit and blue shirt, identified by on-screen text as 'DAVID KAISER MIT', speaking expressively in a lecture hall with chalkboards in the background, his hands animated as he explains that "Science has made enormous progress for centuries by sort of breaking complicated systems down into parts. When we come to phenomena like quantum entanglement, that scheme breaks." A large, glowing sphere resembling a galaxy or nebula appears in a dark, industrial space, followed by a close-up of a hand holding a small, bright light source, as David Kaiser concludes, "When it comes to the bedrock of quantum mechanics, the whole is more than the sum of its parts," with the music intensifying. Anton Zeilinger, with white curly hair and a beard, wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, is shown seated in a lab, speaking thoughtfully with a distinct accent, stating, "The basic motivation is just to learn how nature works. What's really going on? Einstein said it very nicely, he's not interested in this detailed question or that detailed question." The video then shows a man in a dark suit from behind, arms outstretched, holding glowing orbs in each hand, standing in a dimly lit auditorium with warm stage lighting, as the dramatic music continues to play. The PBS | NOVA logo remains visible in the top left corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens on a black screen featuring the PBS | NOVA logo and the bold white title 'EINSTEIN'S QUANTUM RIDDLE'. A male voice speaks, stating, "He just wanted to know what were God's thoughts when he created the world." The title then transitions to a deep blue background displaying major funding sources, including the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation and the John Templeton Foundation, as a sustained, deep, and slightly ominous orchestral score with a sci-fi feel begins. The scene shifts to a dark blue, abstract cosmic background with swirling patterns and faint stars, where 'EINSTEIN'S QUANTUM RIDDLE' reappears with the NOVA logo, and the music transitions to a more dramatic, mysterious orchestral piece. A series of rolling credits follows, listing production roles and institutions like CERN and IBM Research against the cosmic backdrop, with the music evolving into a grand, epic, and cinematic orchestral score. A DVD cover for 'EINSTEIN'S QUANTUM RIDDLE' then appears on the right, showcasing Einstein and observatories along with pricing and purchase information. A female narrator clearly instructs, "To order this program on DVD, visit ShopPBS.org or call 1-800-PLAY-PBS. Episodes of NOVA are available with Passport. NOVA is also available on Amazon Prime Video." The grand orchestral music returns as additional production credits and copyright details, including '© 2018 WGBH Educational Foundation', are displayed. The segment concludes with a promotional screen featuring the NOVA | PBS logo, a call to 'WATCH ON THE PBS VIDEO APP', and social media handles, while the music shifts to a whimsical, almost magical tune.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a black screen featuring the PBS | NOVA logo in the top left corner, followed by the title 'EINSTEIN'S QUANTUM RIDDLE' in bold white text, centered on a black background. The title then transitions to a deep blue background with white text listing major funding sources, including the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation, the John Templeton Foundation, and the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, with the phrase 'Viewers Like You Thank You' displayed to the right. The scene shifts to a dark blue, abstract cosmic background with swirling patterns and faint stars, where the title 'EINSTEIN'S QUANTUM RIDDLE' reappears in white text at the top left, accompanied by the NOVA logo in the bottom right. A series of rolling credits follows, listing production roles such as Director, Producer, Editor, and Narrator, with names like Evan Meyer, Kevin Quantum, and others, all displayed in white text against the same cosmic backdrop. The credits continue with technical roles, archival sources, and special thanks, including institutions like CERN, IBM Research, and the University of California, Berkeley. As the credits progress, a DVD cover for 'EINSTEIN'S QUANTUM RIDDLE' appears on the right side of the screen, showing a portrait of Einstein and observatories, with pricing and purchase information: 'DVD - $24.99 Plus S&H', 'ShopPBS.org', and '1-800-PLAY-PBS', along with logos for PBS Passport and Prime Video. The final frames display additional production credits, including Senior Executive Producer Paula S. Apsell, and legal and rights information, concluding with copyright details: '© 2018 WGBH Educational Foundation' and a note that the program was produced by WGBH, with original funding from Draper, the David H. Koch Fund for Science, and others. The segment ends with a promotional screen featuring the NOVA | PBS logo, a call to 'WATCH ON THE PBS VIDEO APP', and social media handles '@novapbs' with icons for Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram, set against a split white and blue background with the word 'VIEW MORE' in the top right corner.
